author: ap3jedi
message:
AY24/25 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Zimmermann and Prof Anandha
BZA student here. i wont tell you what the mod constitutes as you can look at the other reviews or do your own digging ;). but what i'll offer is my raw and honest review.
As someone who has not ever scored an >A- for the previous CS1010A and CS2030 mods, i went into the mod not having much hope, wishing I could SU it.
Got below mean and median for midterms, failed it, in actual fact. i was crushed... :(
Despite maxxing out the ODAs, THAs and Visualgo quiz, things did not look too good. However, when i realised this might potentially be the last CS coded mod i would ever do, something in me clicked, and i decided to do the best i could.
what i did:
1. grinded about 15 PYPs
2. spam asked chatgpt to help me explain answer keys...
3. attended and listened to all the tutorials attentively (by Prof Roger Zimmermann), asking him questions too.
4. lastly, booked a consult with Prof Anandha during finals season after i did at least 7 papers

author: fkLeetcode
message:
AY25/26, Sem 1
Taught by Profs Sanka Rasnayaka, Roger Zimmermann, Low Kok Lim
Jampacked course, many things to learn (and master) but useful for interviews ig. I got spooked by the reviews here and spent about 3 months during the holidays exclusively starting Leetcode and learning Java before taking this course. This helped greatly and I was able to go through the course, especially the first half, without too much struggle. Despite enjoying CS2030 much more, I scored way better in this course because it’s so smurfable I guess. DP and greedy aren't really the focus here though they may inherently be part of other algorithms like APSP. There are a lot of assessments in this course, which on the one hand means the grade is more spread out, on the other hand very busy . I’ll only highlight the parts of the course that I have anything interesting to note, the rest can refer to other reviews.
Lectures
Start and end was taught by prof sanka, middle part taught by prof roger. In my opinion they are both pretty decent lecturers, but prof roger need to 2x speed him. The course is structured based on some CP4 textbook by prof s Halim .They provide crash course prerecorded lectures to learn Java at the start of the course, but it is wholly insufficient. Java is quite different from Python due to the OOP focus and static typing and being more verbose, so best to familiarise beforehand , I think learning it simultaneously thorugh CS2030 will just cause more stress haha.

author: gomuflower
message:
Taken in AY25/26 S1
Lecturers: Prof Sanka Rasnayaka/Prof Roger Zimmerman
Tutor: Prof Low Kok Lim
Lectures
2 + 1 hours of online lectures each week. Lectures are good though they often overrun quite significantly by up to 1 hour which is quite irritating. Prof Roger lectures quite slowly and rushes at the end and that the slides for the topic on BST/AVL trees are really messy.
Tutorials
The tutorials for this course can be quite challenging as you are expected to apply the data structures and algorithms. My tutor was Prof Low Kok Lim who was good with explaining the concepts but I feel he focused too much on class participation which led to classes overrunning. Overall, tutorials are quite useful for prepping for exams and some of the questions are leetcode-style questions as well :).
Labs (Individual Learning Assignment)
Nothing much to say, besides that it can sometimes be stressful to finish the lab by the end of the lab slot (the newly added requirement this sem). It can also be quite disheartening when your code fails to meet a hidden test case. That said, my tutors and were quite goated and helpful in helping us during the debugging process. Would recommend scheduling your lab slot as late as possible so that you have more time to finish the assignment.

author: Dr Ilyas Bellingham
message:
Taken in AY 25/26
Lecturers: Prof Sanka/ Prof Roger Zimmerman
Programming Language: Java (for assignments), DSA people/people who didnt do CS2030 were told to watch a video produced by the CS2040 teaching team on Java basics (about 3-4 hours long).
Exams: All in pseudo code ie you dont have to write out code in python/java (like they did in CS1010S/E) you can just write the idea in English and it will count.
Assignments:
1. 10 x Lab Assignments (Kattis) (10%) (1.25% each capped at 10% so essentially you only need to do 8 to get the full score)
2. 2 x THA (Kattis) (4%) (2% each, 1% per question)
3. Written Assessment in week 6 (8%)
4. Midterms (25%)
5. Visualgo Quiz 1 and Quiz 2 (8% total)
6. Class participation + Tutorial/Lab attendance - 5%
7. Finals (40%)

author: anonymous_peanut
message:
AY21/22 Sem 2 Prof. Chong Ket Fah
Tutorial Attendance/ Participation - 3%
Lab attendance - 2%
In-lab Assignments - 15%
Take Home Assignments - 12%
Online Quiz 1 - 4%
Online Quiz 2 - 4%
Midterm - 20%
Final Exam - 40%
The in-lab assignments have a one day deadline. During labs, you are asked to show your pseudo-code and your TAs will help with that. Afterwards, you are asked to figure it out yourself. These assignments took me about 1 - 3 hours to complete.
The take home assignments are much tougher than the one day assignments. It took me about a full day to finish each question. Good to get started as soon as the assignments are out.
The online quizzes are conducted in Visualgo. Definitely possible to get full marks with practice as there's only so many formats that the questions are asked.

author: MA1522 UBottom
message:
AY25/26 Semester 1
Lecturers: Roger Zimmerman & Sanka
Tutor: Nguyen
I was expecting CS2040 to be tougher in terms of workload compared to CS2030 based on the past reviews, but I felt like it was much more chill. I believe they reduced the number of THAs and we only had to do two for this semester. The Kattis assignments are also optional and you can frankly skip it, I stopped doing it after the first few weeks. The only downside of CS2040 is that you have to be constantly on your toes to keep up with the almost weekly assessments (there are THAs, Visualgo quizzes, Written assessments etc.). However, the weekly assessments are really chill and easy to score full marks if you can keep up with the lecture contents.
Lectures:
Lectures are 3 hours weekly, split into a two-hour session and a one-hour session on another day. It is quite draining as the two lecturers will usually overrun every single time, so you have to watch the lectures in your own time if you need to head for another class. Otherwise, I thought the explanations by the lecturers are done well but sometimes they focus too much on the basics they end up rushing towards the end.

author: aa
message:
Taken in AY23/24 Sem 2
Lecturer: Prof Chong Ket Fah
Assessments
5% Tutorial/lab attendance
15% ODA (one-day assignments) done in labs
12% THA (take-home assignments)

Content
- Intro to Java

- Sorting algorithms (selection, bubble, insertion, merge, quick, radix)

author: small monkey
message:
AY 22/23 ST2
Prof: Chong Ket Fah
Lab tutor: Wei Tat
Structure:
10x In-Lab Assignment (ILA) - 1 question per lab
8x Take-Home Assignment (THA)- 2 questions per week
2x VisuAlgo quiz
1x Midterms
1x Finals
Workload: 8/10 (for those with no prior java experience)
This mod is very fast paced in special term 2. If you check out other reviews, the module usually has 1 tut and lab per week, and THA are 2 qns biweekly. However for special term, everything is faster by x2 pace. Also, I found myself crapping my pants when I realised that did not really grasp later topics properly. Only during week 6 (7 days before finals) was I confident about my data structures but at that point, I did not have enough practise to see how data structures were used to solve questions (100% my fault)

author: Naman Agrawal
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof. Tan Sun Teck & Prof. Roger Zimmermann
Assessments:
Labs: 12*1% = 12%
Tutorials: 3% Attendance + 3% Participation = 6%
Mock Practical Exam: 3%
Practical Exam: 12%
Visualgo: 12% (5% + 5% + 2% for 3 visualgo quizzes)
Midterms: 15%
Finals: 40%
One of the most important modules for any technical interview. If you like knowing about new problem solving techniques in the form of computational algorithms, you'll enjoy this module. But you'll have to put in lots of efforts! At the start of the module, Prof introduces everyone to the basics of Java and Vim. While Prof does teach the basics, it's vital that you take time to read up some online references too, especially for the labs and practical examination. If you're regular in your lab work, then you wouldn't face a lot of problem on the coding part. Make sure to familiarize yourself with the important Java API data structures and interfaces: Integer, String, Long, Double, Arrays, ArrayList, LinkedList, ArrayDeque, Stack, Queue, HashMap, HashSet, TreeMap, TreeSet, PriotityQueue, et al. By the end of the module you'll probably be playing with many such data structures entangled in a web of custom classes, interface and references.

author: ChlorineCl2
message:
AY22/23 Sem 1
Lecturer: Prof Tan Sun Teck/Roger Zimmermann
Prof Tan for before recess week, Prof Roger for after.
Tutorial: Dani
Lab: Huang Yuqi
Workload is really high.
You have:
Lecture videos
Live lecture Q&A (though after week 3 most stopped attending)
Tutorials
Labs
Mock PE
PE
3 VisualAlgo Quiz
Midterms
Finals
The most stressful ones are the weekly labs even though they only contribute 1% each, They release 3 question, 1 is intended to be submitted. It has to pass every single test case to get that 1%, else 0. On even weeks, it's manually graded for 30% programming style as well. (0.7 for passing all test cases, 0.3 for good programming style). The questions are not easy and take hours to even days to come up with solution. Coding was done in Java.

Overview:
A buildup on cs1010 with a focus on using datastructures and algorithms to solve given tasks as efficiently as possible. In cs1010 variants, most likely you are only concerned with writing programs that solve the given task without much attention to the time/space complexity. However, in this module you would be expected to come up with solutions that run much better than brute force solutions.

author: Damaen Tan
message:
Taken In AY23/24 Semester 2
Lecturer: Prof Chong Ket Fah
Tutor: Ivan Chew
Lab TAs: Tan Si Rui, Issaiah Koh
Assessment Of Module:
Tutorial Attendance (5%)
10 One-day Assignments (15%)
8 Take-Home Assignments (12%)
2 Visualgo Quizzes (8%)
Midterms (20%)
Final Exam (40%)
Content Of Module:
1. Introduction To Java - Java Syntax and Basic Code, Data Types. (Those with CS2030 knowledge can skip this part)
2. Abstract Data Types - Java Interface, Classes in Java
3. Analysis Of Algorithms - Big O Notation, Order of Growth, Analysis of Different Cases.
4. Sorting - Insertion Sort, Bubble Sort, Merge Sort, Selection Sort, Quick Sort, Radix Sort, Bubble Sort with Flag, Time Complexities of these sorting algorithms, stability of sorts, in-place sort.
5. Array Lists and Linked Lists (Including Doubly Linked List, Tailed Linked List, Circular Linked List etc.)
6. Stacks and Queues - FIFO and LIFO algorithms, Palindromes
7. Hash Tables - Hash Functions, Collision Resolution, Separate, Linear and Quadratic Probing, Double Hashing.
8. Binary Heaps - Functions in Binary Heap (Extract Max, Search etc.)
9. UFDS
10. Binary Search Tree (BST) - Ranks, Insertion of Nodes into BST, Deletion of nodes, Successor and Predecessor
11. BST and AVL Tree - BST height and size, balancing BST, Balance Factors, Tree Rotations, Rebalancing
12. Graphs Foundations - Data Structures (Adjacency Matrix, Adjacency List, Edge List)
13. Graph Traversal - BFS and DFS, topological sort (Kahn's Algorithm), Reachability Test, Identifying/Counting Component(s), DAGs, Topological Sort – DFS based algorithm.
14. Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) - Cycle Property, Cut Property, Prim’s Algorithm, Prim’s variant for Dense Graphs, Kruskal’s Algorithm, Sorting Edges in Edge List.
15. Single-Source Shortest Paths (SSSP) - Negative Weight Edges and Cycles, “Relaxation” Operation, BFS for SSSP, BFS on Unweighted Graph, Bellman's Ford Algorithm, Dijkstra's Algorithm, Modified Dijkstra's Algorithm, Lazy Deletion.
16. (NOT TESTED) All-Pairs Shortest Paths (APSP) - Floyd Warshall’s Dynamic Programming algorithm, APSP Solutions with SSSP Algorithms.

author: bellcurvefeeder
message:
AY23/24 Sem 2
Prof: Chong Ket Fah
ODA and THA:
I found the THA extremely tough. Really dreaded whenever 2 weeks passed and the new THA opens up. Advice to take this module with friends, so that can at least "help" one another in some way or another. ODA were generally easier, with one of two being quite tedious. Do make sure that your pseudocode is okay by checking with you lab TA's before embarking on your code, makes it much easier, at least for me.
VisuAlgo Quizzes:
Do study and just keep practicing on VisuAlgo. And always check your answers, do not be over confident since you should really ensure you get full marks on these 2 quizzes.
Midterms:
Midterms involve MCQs/MRQs, analysis questions and application questions. I found the midterms very tough. During midterms, I feel that since fewer topics are covered, they test a lot more "in depth" questions and really required much thinking. Structure was also different from PYP's, which caught me off-guard.

author: កម្ពុជា
message:
Taken 23/24 Sem 1
Prof: Chong Ket Fah
Workload: 9/10
Difficulty: 100/10
Grade: B+
The mod teaches data structures and algorithms in Java. The midterm and final only test on pseudocode and Java is used only in Labs and Assignment. Lectures are entirely online through zoom and they are also recorded. Thought I understand the lectures quite well but don't know why it doesn't translate to actually knowing how to solve questions. I guess I should have practiced more.
A note to self: don't overload with CS mods or you might die. ☠️

author: RandomDSAguy
message:
Taken in AY23/24 Semester 1
Under Prof Chong Ket Fah
Assessments:
Take Home Assignments (THAs): 12%
One Day Assignments (ODAs): 15%
Visualgo Quizzes: 8%
Lab + Tutorial Attendance: 5%
Midterm: 20%
Finals: 40%
Overall:
Quite an interesting course that covers various algorithms and data structures. It’s very mind blowing to see that some algorithms just work. I would recommend everyone to take this mod with their friends especially when it is under Prof Chong Ket Fah.
ODA:
As the name suggests, it’s a one day (coding) assignment on Kattis (something like coursemology). For this semester, it started in Friday morning and ended on Saturday morning. There’s a lab on friday for everyone so the lab TAs can help us with the ODA. Out of the 13 weeks of school, there are 10 weeks when you have to do this. However you don’t have to worry because if you actually follow lecture and seek help from your TA, it should be quite doable. This is also where your group of friend might come in because I received so much support from them. It shouldn’t be hard but expect your friday afternoon/evening to be gone because of this. I know people who can solve it during Lab itself also.

author: kfcemployee
message:
AY 23/24 S1
Prof: Chong Ket Fah
Lab Tutors: Jeremy Lim and Nicholas Tng
Structure:
10 One Day Assignments - submit within 24 hours on the day of lab
4 Take Home Assignments - 2 questions every 2 weeks
2 Visualgo Quiz
Midterms
Finals
Workload: 10/10
This mod takes up more time compared all 4 of my other mods. If you are relatively new to coding and your only prior experience was CS1010 like me then prepare to suffer. Java is an awful language. Not only do you have to learn how to use Java, you also need to be studying the content of the mod. It feels like an insane amount of work is needed to do well for this mod. I spent roughly 3-4 hours on the One Day Assignments EVEN with help from my TA. And Take Home Assignments roughly 4-5 days per problem, just thinking of how to tackle it. I swear my peanut brain is not meant for coding man.

author: kok
message:
Taken in AY21/22 ST 2 under Dr Chong Ket Fah
NOTE: I took this under the RNSMen Special Term programme after completing CS1010X, and this review will mostly be tailored to future incoming freshmen who are considering doing the same.
Summary:
Consider carefully if you want to do this under the RNSMen ST programme. It WILL clash with orientations (I spent 8 of the last 10 days before the finals at orientation camps), and it WILL be fairly time-consuming (if nothing else because of the graded attendance/participation components). I juggled a full-time internship + this module + attending all the relevant orientation camps - my performance in all 3 along with my mental and physical health suffered quite badly. For my batch, all the CS1010X -> CS2040 students made a Telegram group together for academic and moral support. I highly recommend that future batches do the same.
Special Note on Module Planning: There will be a note on the Special Term 2 registration saying that “Computer Science Undergraduates cannot take CS2040”. If you are an RNSMan NG student majoring in Computer Science, you can still take CS2040 in Special Term because you aren’t an Undergraduate yet :). For Computer Science majors, taking this lets you do your Data Structures and Algorithms module 2 semesters ahead, since once you matriculate you must do CS2040S which has CS1231/S as a prerequisite, while CS2040 only needs CS1010-equivalent. This lets you do funny things like take CS2220 or CS2105 in Year 1 Semester 1, and CS3233, CS3230, CS2103T, CS3241, CS4236, CS5342 in Year 1 Semester 2 (which other CS1010-exempt students can’t). If you are an EE/CEG major, you may have to wrestle with department admin staff who may get annoyed that you didn’t do Data Structures and Algorithms in C++ (CS2040C), but it should work out in the end.

author: dogshit_cs_student
message:
Taken in AY2021/2022 Special Term(Part 2) under Prof Chong Ket Fah
Disclaimer: I am a student of dogshit-calibre so if you are reading this chances are you will be able to cope better than me. In fact my previous review got flagged for spam so Im guessing the quality of reviews is correlated to my quality as a soc student.
I do not recommend taking this before matriculating if you have cleared 1010X, unless you're a programming god and already know most or at least half of the content. Do note that orientation activities fall on the last few weeks of this module in special term as well. In fact I would not recommend taking this module in special term at all, considering the amount of content that it covers and that there are only 6 weeks.
This is not a complaint about prof's teaching or the syllabus but 6 weeks is just too short and painful. For a module that people emphasise the importance of, 6 weeks is just not enough to absorb all the content taught.
Fun fact the module started with 98 students and at the end only 76 remained.
For course content, you can take a look at kok's review. Each week consists of 2 days of classes. 3 hour lecture + 1 hour tutorial + 1hour++ lab session for the ODA. Do note that there were classes up till the day before finals, the schedule is that packed. Midterms on week 3, finals on week 6.

Assessment: 30% CA, 20% midterms, 50% finals. CA is comprised of 2 one day assignments worth 1% every week and 1 one week take home worth 2%, 2 Visualgo quizzes worth 8% and tutorial/lab participation.
All content was covered within 5 weeks. Don't take this module in special term, it is too hectic and too fast. I learnt more from online youtube videos explaining DS&A than from the lecturer. author: awake
message:
Taken in AY18/19 Sem 1
Lecturer: Asst Prof Harold Soh
Synopsis: CS2040 introduces fundamental data structures and algorithms to students, as well as some basic asymptotic analysis. The module is taught in Java and assumes basic working knowledge of Java.
Assessment: Comprised of weekly/fortnightly take-home problemsets (30%), two quizzes (6%), a coding quiz (4%), a midterm (10%), participation (10%) and a final exam (40%). Weekly/fortnightly problemsets are interesting and are for putting concepts into practice, and are not difficult to do well in, given that collaboration is encouraged and the long deadline. The two quizzes serve as evaluations of learning progress in the module and are not difficult with a good understanding of the module content. The coding quiz is a practical exam and there's a lot of time for you figure out what to do (the problemsets should prepare you for this). The midterm and finals do not focus on how exactly the data structures and algorithms work, but how they can be applied to solve various algorithmic problems, and so exposure to a variety of problems will help. Finally, in-class and forum participation are encouraged and will count towards the participation component of the final grade.
Lecturer: Prof Harold is an excellent lecturer with a great flair for teaching. He engages the audience well and lectures are generally interesting, even if you have prior knowledge.

author: monikernemo
message:
Review for 17/18 Sem 2
Lecturer: AP Tan Sun Teck
Assessment:
18% Sit in Lab
2% Lab Attendance
5% Take Home Lab
15% Practical Exam
5% Tutorial Attendance
15% Midterm
40% Finals
This is an introductory module on Data Structures (linked list, stack, queue, binary trees, heap etc) and Algorithms (primarily focusing on sorting and some graph traversal problems). The content isn't inherently difficult because most, if not all, of the idea are quite intuitive. Despite being a veteran lecturer of CS1020, 2010, Prof Tan's implementation of the module makes the course difficult in some ways (most notably by having sit-in lab assssment once every two weeks is hellish and topped off with us struggling to learning to use Java properly).
The pacing of teaching was also quite off, with him spending too much time in the morning simpler topics at the start and mad Sprint towards the end where more complicated and involved topics were discussed and could have been more beneficial if more time were spent on those.
